Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase + object + result clause (serial verb construction)
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Carelessness or complacency can lead to serious consequences.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Being careless can cause serious harm.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in plantation-era survival awareness where alertness was necessary for safety.
First Example Sentence: Yu betta wake up, tek sleep mark death inna dis ya place.
Second Example Sentence: Dem bin tek sleep mark death an los di whole contract.
Third Example Sentence: Inna politics, tek sleep mark death quick quick.

Idiom (Cham): When breeze blow, fowl bottom expose
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional subordinate clause + main clause metaphor
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The truth is revealed when circumstances change.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Difficult times reveal people’s true character.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Agrarian imagery reflecting village life and observation of animals.
First Example Sentence: Wait likl, when breeze blow, fowl bottom expose.
Second Example Sentence: Election season show how breeze blow an fowl bottom expose.
Third Example Sentence: Trouble come an fowl bottom expose right away.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative copular clause with plural subject
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Not everyone is the same; differences are natural.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: People have different abilities and qualities.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Body metaphor common in West African and Caribbean oral traditions.
First Example Sentence: Member all finger naa equal, so treat dem fair.
Second Example Sentence: Inna class, all finger naa equal.
Third Example Sentence: Manager seh all finger naa equal when he promote she.

Idiom (Cham): Yu cyaan plant yam an expect cassava
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative modal construction + coordinated verb phrase
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: You cannot expect a different result from what you put in.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Outcomes depend on the effort or input given.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Agricultural roots tied to subsistence farming traditions.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Cham): /ju kjaːn plant jam an ɪkspɛkt kasava/
First Example Sentence: Yu cyaan plant yam an expect cassava, study harder.
Second Example Sentence: He lazy but want promotion; yu cyaan plant yam an expect cassava.
Third Example Sentence: Dem invest small an want big profit—plant yam an expect cassava?

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone acting above rules or authority.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Acting as if above the law or rules.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Postcolonial institutional critique reflecting distrust of authority.
First Example Sentence: He move like he bigga dan di system.
Second Example Sentence: Nobody bigga dan di system inna dis country.
Third Example Sentence: She talk bold like she bigga dan di system.

Idiom (Cham): Tek time mek monkey climb tree
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Be patient and allow time for progress.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Animal imagery common in Afro-Caribbean folklore.
First Example Sentence: Relax, tek time mek monkey climb tree.
Second Example Sentence: Business slow but tek time mek monkey climb tree.
Third Example Sentence: Yu rushing too much; tek time mek monkey climb tree.

Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Too much of something can cause problems.
First Example Sentence: Careful how much yu spend—too much wata drown pumpkin.
Second Example Sentence: She smother di pikni; too much wata drown pumpkin.
Third Example Sentence: Overtraining athletes prove too much wata drown pumpkin.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Descriptive adjective phrase borrowed and localized
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Maritime culture and fishing economy influence.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Borrowed idiom adapted into Creole phonology.
